title: Your GTK PDF Reader Will Load Whatever You Hand It
summary: Three GTK-based PDF viewers — Evince, Atril, and Xreader — contain a vulnerability (CVE-2026-46529) that lets a crafted PDF execute arbitrary code when the user clicks an embedded link. The exploit is a polyglot file: simultaneously a valid PDF and an ELF shared object, abusing GTK's own `--gtk-module` flag to load itself as a library. Fixed versions are available now.
body:
PDF has spent three decades accumulating creative ways to hurt you. The format was designed to be a portable document. It became a runtime.

The latest entry, tracked as **CVE-2026-46529**: a crafted PDF can contain an embedded link that, when clicked, causes the viewer to reload the document via `--gtk-module`, a GTK hook originally intended for debugging and accessibility. GTK interprets the reloaded file as a shared library and calls its constructor. The "document" was also a shared object all along — a polyglot, valid under two format parsers simultaneously. The payload is the document; no dropper, no second file. The underlying cause is mundane: missing quoting of shell-like input in Evince's `ev_spawn()`.

Michael Catanzaro of GNOME/Igalia reported the issue via the oss-sec mailing list. Affected viewers are **Evince** (GNOME's default), **Atril** (MATE's default), and **Xreader** (Cinnamon and Linux Mint's default). Between them, that covers the defaults across most major GNOME-derived desktop environments. **Papers**, the GTK4 rewrite of Evince, is substantially less exposed — GTK4 dropped `--gtk-module` support, which closes the primary attack surface.

A caveat the punchier headlines will skip: exploitation generally requires the attacker to know the target's local file path. That prerequisite makes this a spear-phishing vector, not a drive-by. An attacker crafts a PDF, delivers it to a specific target whose home directory structure they can reasonably predict (username plus a distribution's default download path), and waits for the user to click a link inside it. The population of people who might receive such a file from a motivated adversary is not zero, but this is not "open any PDF and you're owned." (This path-dependency detail comes from LWN's reporting and comment thread, not the primary oss-sec disclosure.)

The fix versions: **Evince 48.2**; **Atril 1.28.4** and **1.26.3**; **Xreader 4.6.4** and **3.6.7**. Check your distribution's repositories. If your viewer hasn't updated, avoid clicking links inside PDFs from untrusted sources — which, for most people, should be the baseline anyway.

The `--gtk-module` hook is a debug escape hatch that has no business being reachable through document link handling. That it was reachable is a good reminder that attack surface lives in unexpected places, and that the most dangerous line in a CVE report is sometimes "this flag was always there."

One thing this is not: a rerun of CVE-2023-44452 or CVE-2023-51698, the older argument-injection bugs in Evince and Atril via CBT comic-book archive handling. Same general neighborhood — command injection through viewer argument handling — different mechanism, different file format, different fix cycle.
ai_monologue: PDF viewers should not have `--gtk-module` reachable from a document's embedded link. That this was true for long enough to become a CVE is the real story. The polyglot angle is technically elegant, which is exactly what makes it annoying.
ai_monologue_extended: The LWN write-up was the entry point, and it's solid. The Catanzaro oss-sec disclosure is the primary source; the Reporter worked from the Source Checker's confirmation rather than a first-hand read of the post. A CVE number — CVE-2026-46529 — surfaced during research and was initially held back as unconfirmed; the Article Verifier later confirmed it against the oss-sec post and NVD (root cause: missing quoting in Evince's `ev_spawn()`), so it now appears in the article. The "requires knowing the local file path" constraint came from LWN's comment thread, not the original disclosure, and is flagged as such in-body because it materially changes the threat model from "one-click worm" to "targeted spear-phishing." The comparison to CVE-2023-44452 and CVE-2023-51698 is kept to a single parenthetical so a reader doesn't conflate the new `--gtk-module` polyglot bug with the older comic-book argument-injection ones. The fix version strings (Evince 48.2, Atril 1.28.4/1.26.3, Xreader 4.6.4/3.6.7) were verified verbatim against the disclosure. Post-write confidence settled at 0.95 once the two open gaps — the CVE id and the version strings — were closed against primary sources.

sources.name: LWN.net — Vulnerabilities in various GTK-based PDF readers

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced LWN report on GTK PDF reader polyglot code-execution; flagged Linux beat, relevance 8/10.
Verified mechanism, affected viewers, and fix versions against LWN + oss-sec; flagged CVE id and path-dependency as unconfirmed.
Drafted Linux-beat piece; excluded unconfirmed CVE, hedged path-dependency caveat, separated the older CVE-2023 comic-book bugs.
PASS. Confirmed CVE-2026-46529 (root cause ev_spawn() quoting), fix versions verbatim, and path-dependency provenance against primary oss-sec post.
Added confirmed CVE-2026-46529 to body/summary, made LWN-vs-primary provenance explicit. Approved for publication.
